title: Tirokafteri
subtitle: Spicy Greek feta and roasted pepper dip
excerpt: Tirokafteri is a sharp, spicy Greek dip of crumbled feta, roasted red pepper, chilli, and olive oil. It takes five minutes to make and keeps for three days in the fridge. Serve with bread or as part of a mezze spread.
sourceNotes: Tirokafteri means 'fiery cheese' in Greek. It is a staple of Macedonian and northern Greek cooking, typically made by mashing together feta and chilli with little else. The roasted pepper version is common in household and taverna kitchens.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps well in the fridge for 3 days. The flavour sharpens with time.

content.text: Tirokafteri is a Greek dip of feta, roasted red pepper, chilli, and olive oil. It is quick to make and has a salty, spicy punch that cuts through richer foods on a mezze spread. The heat level is adjustable; start with one chilli and add more to taste.

attrs.title: Roasting the pepper
attrs.body: Hold a red pepper directly over a gas flame or place under a very hot grill, turning until the skin is blackened all over. Put in a covered bowl for 10 minutes, then peel away the skin. Alternatively, use a jarred roasted red pepper, drained and patted dry.

content.text: Place the crumbled feta, roasted pepper, chilli, olive oil, and lemon juice in a food processor. Pulse until roughly combined. For a chunkier dip, pulse just a few times; for a smooth dip, blend for longer. Taste and add more chilli, lemon, or a pinch of salt if needed.
If using yoghurt, stir it in by hand after blending. Transfer to a serving bowl and drizzle with a little more olive oil.

items.cause: Feta is naturally salty and the quantity varies between brands
items.fix: Stir in 2 tablespoons of Greek yoghurt to soften the saltiness. Do not add extra salt until you taste the finished dip.
Add olive oil a teaspoon at a time and pulse between additions until you reach the consistency you want.

content.text: Tirokafteri is most strongly associated with northern Greece and Macedonia, where feta is eaten in large quantities and chilli features more prominently than in southern cooking. It appears on mezze spreads alongside olives, taramasalata, and tzatziki. A bowl of it in the fridge is useful with almost anything.
